statusLine: European fuel security remains under strain with 16 of 27 countries below mandatory stock minimums and oil prices up 27% since February tensions.
fullAnalysis:
European fuel security shows persistent vulnerability despite some improvement in average stock levels. While EU-wide petrol reserves at 96.3 days exceed the 90-day mandate, diesel (88.8 days) and jet fuel (87.3 days) remain below required thresholds. Most concerning is the widespread nature of shortfalls: 16 of 27 countries fail to meet the 90-day minimum for at least one fuel type, with several facing critical shortages across multiple categories.

The geographical pattern reveals particular strain in Central and Southern Europe. Poland, Austria, Romania, and Luxembourg face critical shortages across all three fuel types, while major economies like Italy and France struggle with petrol and jet fuel reserves. The jet fuel situation appears most precarious, with 17 countries below the 90-day threshold, reflecting ongoing aviation sector pressures from Middle East supply disruptions.

Fuel prices reflect underlying supply tensions, with Brent crude at $92.16 per barrel — a 27% increase since late February escalation. The €1.79-1.80 average for both petrol and diesel across the EU masks significant regional variation, with Danish consumers paying €2.39/L while Malta enjoys €1.34/L. This price divergence, combined with uneven stock distributions, highlights the fragmented nature of European energy security despite common policy frameworks.
keyPoints: 16 of 27 EU countries fail to meet 90-day minimum reserves for at least one fuel type, with Luxembourg, Poland, Austria, and Romania in critical shortage across all fuels
Jet fuel reserves most vulnerable with 17 countries below mandate, averaging just 87.3 days EU-wide
Oil prices up 27% since February Middle East tensions ($72.48 to $92.16/barrel), driving fuel costs to €1.79-1.80/L average
Major economies affected: Italy critical on all fuels, France struggling with petrol and jet fuel, Germany below mandate on jet fuel only
Eastern Europe particularly strained, while Nordic and Baltic states maintain stronger reserve positions
